source: Invest Like the Best — AI, semiconductors, and the robotic frontier (EP.385)
summary: Non-humanoid robotics startups will not produce category-defining outcomes; incumbents with data, compute, and capital will dominate
summary_extended: Baker argues that non-humanoid robotics startups will fail to produce category-defining, Google-scale venture outcomes, instead achieving at best mediocre results analogous to Lycos, CMGI, or Myspace — companies that existed in the right era but were ultimately displaced by better-resourced incumbents. His reasoning is that the same structural advantages that allowed large incumbents to dominate large language models — proprietary data, massive compute, and deep capital — apply equally to robotics, making it nearly impossible for underfunded startups to compete long-term. He is explicitly negative on investing in specialized non-humanoid robot startups as category winners, viewing the space as a venture trap. No specific incumbent beneficiaries are named in this thesis segment, and Baker does not identify which large companies will win, only that startups will lose.
detail: Baker explicitly predicts non-humanoid robot startups will at best achieve 'Lycos or CMGI or Myspace type venture outcomes' but not Google-scale outcomes. The same dynamics that advantage incumbents in LLMs (data, compute, capital) apply to robotics. He recommends against investing in specialized robot startups as category winners.
confidence_evidence: Baker makes an explicit negative prediction about the category and draws a direct historical analogy
quote_fragment: good luck to all these non-humanoid startup robot companies
